[[RunningHead]]

Erratum

In the Presidential Address entitled "The Present Status of Organized Psychoanalysis in the United States," by Robert P. Knight, M.D., which appeared in Volume I, Number 2, 1953 of the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, reference was made to the fact that the Detroit Psychoanalytic Institute was established in 1940. This is an error. It was established in 1947. From 1940 to 1947 a Detroit Training Center existed under the supervision of the Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Studies on the Nature of Suggestion—Part I Experimental Induction of Dreams by Direct Suggestion
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Studies on the Nature of Suggestion—Part I Experimental Induction of Dreams by Direct Suggestion11

Charles Fisher Ph.D.

Charles Fisher

Charles Fisher  

Thirty years ago Freud (12) commented that, "Now that I once more approach the riddle of suggestion after having kept away from it for some thirty years, I find there is no change in the situation." Earlier Ferenczi (6) had shown that the capacity to be influenced by suggestion depends upon the possibility of transference taking place, that is, upon the reactivation of repressed, infantile erotic attitudes toward the parent figures. Freud (12) later defined suggestion as a conviction which is not based upon perception and reasoning but upon an erotic tie. He advanced our theoretical understanding in his conception that in suggestion sexual tendencies are inhibited in their aims and the object takes the place of the ego ideal. Jones (16) developed this latter conception further when he showed that in suggestion there is a regression from the ego ideal to the earlier stage of narcissistic identification with the parent. In the thirty years since Freud made his last contribution to the subject, the theory of suggestion has been relatively neglected in spite of considerable work in the field of hypnotism.

Nunberg (23) once made the statement that the place to study hypnosis is in the analytic transference situation. I was much struck by this seemingly paradoxical remark, since it pointed up the similarities between the two states or relationships.

1From the Department of Psychiatry, The Mount Sinai Hospital, New York City.2Assistant Attending Psychiatrist, The Mount Sinai Hospital, New York City.222

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Treatment of a Man Suffering with Ulcerative Colitis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Treatment of a Man Suffering with Ulcerative Colitis11

Mary Cushing M.D.

Mary Cushing

Mary Cushing  

Several years ago a patient came to the author seeking analytic treatment for the ulcerative colitis from which he suffered. Neither at that time nor since did the literature yield much that was conclusive as to theory or helpful in the conduct of therapy. A fair number of authors warned against the use of analytic methods for one reason or another.

In this paper the attempt is made to evaluate experiences with this patient in the light of what was reported as well as what appeared to be crucial situations in the patient's illness and recovery.

The first contact with him was through a letter he had written to the referring psychiatrist whom he had seen in consultation. In it he stated that he had had numerous attacks of ulcerative colitis but was then in a remission. His brother, who suffered from the same disease, was under the care of an analyst in another city and had been more or less symptom-free for several years. He himself had been told by his proctologist that he was "inordinately tense," and psychiatric consultation was recommended. He was seen a few days later, in May 1945.

He was a tall, good-looking man, meticulously dressed, extremely courteous, but stern, almost haughty in expression and manner. He brought with him two documents, his time schedule and a chronological table of the history of his illness.

1Read at the Midwinter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December, 1951.510

responseSet.title: Reconstruction and Screen Function
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reconstruction and Screen Function11

Norman Reider M.D.

Norman Reider

Norman Reider  

SUMMARY

Problems connected with the effects of reconstruction have been considered. Case material is presented wherein the reconstruction was followed by an aggravation of symptoms and by recall of an episode, in one sense derivative of the original traumatic event, in another sense serving as a screen function. The hypothesis is, therefore, proposed that the associative connection between constructions and recollections of derivative screen material results from the fact that the reconstruction offered by the analyst serves as a command to remember, following the mechanisms described by Fenichel on the inner injunction to remember.

The reconstruction, when it has the double effect described, then permits, by its approximate accuracy, unconscious material to reach consciousness, but via derivatives which serve as a screen. The

404

other effect is in the aggravation of symptoms, which likewise serves a resistance function. Coupled together, the double effect may be understood as a compromise solution to a disturbance in economic relationships precipitated by a reconstruction.

[[RunningHead]]

Bibliography of Early Psychoanalytic Monographic Series—2—secondary, Publisher-Sponsored Series88

Ilse Bry Ph.D. , Helen Bayne and Myrl Ebert

Myrl Ebert

Myrl Ebert  

In the preceding bibliography4 we listed the four primary monographic series which began in 1907, 1910, 1914 and 1924, and which were marked by two distinguishing features: (1) they were edited personally by, or under the direction of, Sigmund Freud, and (2) the writings included made their first published appearance in this form.

In this second step toward identification of the psychoanalytic classics we deal with a very different type of series. From 1919 on, these were sponsored by the publisher, Internationaler Psychoanalytischer Verlag. With the aid of a postwar fund, dedicated by its Hungarian donors to cultural purposes and, specifically, to psychoanalysis, Freud had just founded this nonprofit publishing enterprise and announced its functions: directed by Otto Rank, it would ensure publication of the two psychoanalytic periodicals of the period, the Internationale Zeitschrift für ärztliche Psychoanalyse and the Imago; expedite the printing of books and pamphlets bearing upon psychoanalysis, both medical and applied; and take better care of the authors' interests than was customary on the part of the commercial book dealer-publishers.5

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

IV. Problems of Hypertension

Maurice Levine M.D. and Nathan Ackerman M.D.

Nathan Ackerman

Nathan Ackerman  

There was a tendency among the speakers at the Round Table to demand a critical re-examination of the prevailing scientific theories as to the relations between emotion and the so-called psychosomatic disorders. According to them, "getting your blood pressure up" and hypertension as a disease process may be two entirely different things. The disease process, hypertension, ought not be confused with one of its clinical signs, namely, elevated blood pressure. The phenomenon of vasomotor instability should perhaps be distinguished from the disease itself. Challenging favored hypotheses even further, some of the participants in the Round Table wondered whether rage, repressed or otherwise, had any specific relation whatever to hypertension. Questions were raised at to whether Cannon's concepts of emotion and bodily change, the "fight or flight" theory, were being misconstrued. Finally, it was asserted that while increased peripheral resistance in the arterioles was basic to hypertensive disease, the pathophysiology of this condition was not yet clear, and hence it is not possible to attempt a correlation with specific psychic states.

In an introductory statement, Maurice Levine stressed the need to bring psychoanalysis into closer relation with medical research and treatment. He expressed the hope that an alert group could expose gaps in our knowledge and stimulate the formulation of critical research designs. The relative newness of this type of panel at a psychoanalytic meeting provides some special interest. What is the potential of psychoanalysis for a significant contribution to the whole problem of somatization of anxiety?

As against an anticipated offering of perhaps six short papers, there were in all ten separate contributions. I shall endeavor to give each a place in this report. Since the subject matter of these contributions is so vast in scope, I shall take the liberty of making selective references to these papers in accordance with their pertinence to certain basic and controversial issues in this field.

responseSet.title: Past and Present in the Transference
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Past and Present in the Transference11

Mark Kanzer M.D.

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer  

The genetic approach—the reconstruction of past experience and of its influence on the present—is a basic feature of the theory and practice of psychoanalytic therapy. The aims and methods involved, however, have changed throughout the years. Originally the intention was to recapture specific forgotten memories that were traumatically embedded in the unconscious. By making these accessible to consciousness, the mind was to be purged of disturbing elements that produced symptoms.

At present, we understand that a complicated character formation has intervened between the forgotten episodes of early childhood and the contemporary disturbances. The total personality, not isolated incidents, must be visualized in a context of total experience as the research into the past is pushed. The technique by which this is accomplished is psychoanalysis itself.

The investigation of the past goes hand in hand, in analysis, with the study of immediate events in the life of the patient. It is through its effect upon the present that the influence of the past is felt. It is through the pressure of the present that the memories of the past are set in action. The analyst must therefore keep his attention constantly focused on a screen against which are projected two sets of images—from the past and from the present. His task requires that he concentrate neither on one nor on the other alone, but rather on the relation between them. The normal individual spontaneously fuses the past and present images that are operative within himself; the neurotic blurs and interchanges them.

1Presented before the New York Psychoanalytic Society, March 11, 1952.144

Bibliography of Early Psychoanalytic Monographic Series—I—under the Editorship of Freud11

Ilse Bry Ph.D. , Helen Bayne and Myrl Ebert

Myrl Ebert

Myrl Ebert  

Half a century ago and through the 1920's publication of books in the new field of psychoanalysis was difficult. The workers were few and the reading public was limited, and a large edition entailed more risk than publishers cared to assume. According to Dr. Jacob Shatzky, Librarian of the New York State Psychiatric Institute and Hospital, psychoanalysts who wished to get their ideas publicized in a form other than periodical articles underwrote small editions of books which they grouped under over-all series titles such as Internationale Psychoanalytische Bibliothek and Imago-Bücher. For example, each of the nine books cited among the nearly 140 items in the bibliography of Karl Abraham form a part of one or another of these series.

Even some of Freud's own works first appeared in this form, and as early as 1907 Freud began such a monographic series, with himself as editor: Schriften zur angewandten Seelenkunde. For the first edition of the opening issue Freud wrote the following "Announcement" which is not found in any other presentation of his writings:

The Schriften zur angewandten Seelenkunde,

responseSet.title: On the Psychodynamics of Teasing
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Psychodynamics of Teasing11

Samuel Sperling M.D.

Samuel Sperling

Samuel Sperling  

SUMMARY

A simplified schematic picture of the teasing act is represented in the chart. The solid black arrow signifies the predominant tendency of the teaser toward an activity which is painful, hostile, serious, and antagonistic. This induces an element of reluctant participation in the teasee, whose primary tendency, represented by the open arrow, is the opposite—friendly, playful, pleasurable, and co-operative. To induce and secure this volitional interplay, the teaser, consciously and partially inhibits his aggression, though not usually up to the given impression. The teasee would not be drawn into or remain in the interactivity without the inducement of a pleasure premium. The ratio of the primary tendencies of each in any specific interplay is indicated by a point on the slanting line. This is crudely designated by the order of the synonyms used at corresponding positions. Thus, the greater the influence of the teaser is to that of the teasee, the greater the hostile over the friendly component contained in the teasing (see chart).

When the teaser's sadistic impulses are insufficiently curbed, the interplay tends to lose its teasing quality. As this interactivity passes beyond the anger, temper, or cry points, it becomes solely a fighting, bullying or tormenting engagement. This reflects a change in status from being teased to being tormented, from an active, volitional participant to an unwilling recipient. The loss of teasing quality can result similarly from the removal of the teaser's thwarting aim to the elicited impulses of the teasee. What follows then is a relatively unambivalent, harmless, competitive game, a friendly rivalry or a pleasurable, co-operative interplay. The absense of either participant's inhibiting tendency nebulizes the essential ambitendent character of teasing. These elements demarcate the boundaries of teasing and are indicated on the chart by a zone to represent its variability.

The curbing of the teaser's sadistic impulses is facilitated by the satisfactions to be derived from the playful component of teasing, and by the teasee's defensive use of thwarting directed against the fulfillment of the sadistic aim. The teasee's withdrawal tendencies are restrained in proportion to the gratification to be derived from aggressively thwarting, minimizing or counteracting the teaser's

476

hostility, from the masochistic expression and from the positive achievement of converting the teasing to a friendlier interplay. Through the counteracting of each contestant's initial, predominant tendencies and the maintenance of their drives within teasing bounds, antisocial inclinations are mastered. Substantial gratification of instinctual drives is thereby achieved within the confines of increasing mutual acceptability and tolerance. Teasing and its derivatives provide and evolutionary series of socializing experiences which influence the molding of instinctual drives into distinctive personality patterns.

It is difficult for parents to accept the intense, often physically aggressive forms of teasing in early sibling rivalry as a constructive step in the healthy channeling of hostile, jealous feelings. Nevertheless, teasing enables the child to master its primitive, sadomasochistic impulses by affording these a restrained, repetitive, and sometimes persistent outlet. This lessening in the intensity, directness, and urgency of instinctual drives permits a greater psychic tolerance for the inhibited quantity of remaining instinctual tension.

Aggression in teasing is also expressed in the specific form of thwarting the impulses of one's opponent. By identification with a frustrating aggressor, the teaser, through repetitive thwarting activities, attempts to master his own passively sustained, painful frustrations. The teasee may use the interplay similarly in his attempt to thwart the teaser's aggression. Teasing is rather unique in the psychic economy of the child in that the interplay per se revolves about the intentional aim and activity of frustrating. Through teasing, painful experiences of frustration are replayed in less disturbing or somewhat pleasurable form. Frustration per se, thereby, becomes libidinized, and mastered through early teasing.

In teasing, the ego develops mastery of sadomasochistic drives by partly (a) inhibiting, (b) directly expressing, and (c) converting them into playful pleasurable activities.7 This economic solution

477

of such instincts in teasing promotes their social acceptability.

This essential psychodynamic and social value, or so-called "aim" of teasing, should not be confused with any original, antisocial aim of the contestants. The initial hostile or escaping tendencies of teasing suffer incompleteness of expression or thwarting. Teasing fades off into other kinds of activities or breaks off abruptly and is characterized by this kind of incompleteness and nonfulfillment. The resultant changed activities are represented in the chart outside the bounds of the teasing interplay.

An understanding of teasing involves not only that of volitional participative responsiveness, but also that of withdrawal reactions to being teased. These may be of true or false character or a mixture of these, as indicated in the chart. The point at which a teasee withdraws from a teasing experience reflects his tolerance of the magnitude of the implied aggression and/or often the vulnerability of sensitive areas of his personality. To withdraw from the interactivity is normal when the aggression threatens to become excessive and the teasing loses its friendly playfulness. If the "teased" one cannot protect himself against the persistent tormentor in a tantalizing or tormenting experience, he may withdraw into a stuporous state, as reported in the son of the patient cited, and in the Balinese children (1).

Sulking is that type of withdrawal to which the teased one resorts in order simultaneously to convey his willingness to resume the interactivity under less distressing conditions. Sulking contributes to that common pattern of interpersonal relationships of sensitive beings whose aloofness and reserve assert their overt social independence, but whose unresolved dependency needs are manifested in their becoming "hangers on" of voluble, aggressive and sociable friends.

Sometimes in withdrawal reactions, false suffering is displayed by pseudo tantrum, magnified or mock injury or crying. Such exaggerated displays of suffering may serve to promote the retaliatory punishment or disapproval of the teaser by the parents, group, or other supportive figures, or to stimulate the teaser to greater suppression of his aggression. Behavioral techniques of pseudo withdrawal are designed to secure more satisfying interpersonal

478

relationships by the teased one. These may range and evolve from those of marked negativistic nature as sulking, petulance, pouting, and peevishness through experiences of inducing cajoling, coaxing, wheedling, and coddling to those more positive, provocative types of distant aloofness or enticement, as coquettishness, coyness, and challenging demureness.

Speculations upon the genesis of teasing interplay leads to the common tickling game of infancy. This represents one of the earliest nonanaclitic interactivities, in which the infant enters into the spirit of interplay. Not the repetitious tickling act, which may be annoying and even painful, but the playful uncertainty, resulting from admixtures to it of unfulfilled threats and pretense to tickle, elicits from the infant outbursts of laughter. Such tickling play has, in common with teasing, the following basic components: interactive participation in eliciting behavior, uncertainty, pretense, unpleasant potentialities and the pleasure premium of the latter's nonfulfillment.

Anal-erotic conflicts of giving or holding back to an eliciting or demanding person, or of expressing expulsive and retentive propensities in a sadomasochistic relationship, appear to provide the main zonal substratum of the teasing interplay. Impulses other than sadomasochistic ones, especially strong exhibitionistic tendencies or desires for physical contact, may find expression in demonstrations of one's superiority, prowess, or attractiveness.

Of special significance is that contribution of the anal phase in which the fear of expressing bitter resentment may be resolved by the disguised expression of genuine defiance under a seeming artful compliance. This duality pattern simulates the parental example of obtaining the child's compliance to give forthrightly its valuable production on the pot under the friendly guise of cajolery, playful co-operation or storytelling. When, shortly thereafter, a similar expression in soiling arouses their ridicule, mocking or teasing, the child may feel painfully bewildered, tricked and humiliated. This reversal of attitudes, with revelation of latent, hostile feelings, leads the disappointed child to withdraw from open, direct encounter with such two-faced figures. The disillusioned child's ability to return to such interrelationships is

479

facilitated by the defense of simulation and play against the genuine and serious, acquired in the testing experience of teasing. The "duplicity" experience with the parents, if frequent, may give rise to a hard, isolated core of distrust and cynicism toward a world that tantalizes, that beckons, then disappoints. Attempts to disprove fear of painful disappointment in similar interrelationships gives a counterphobic defensive quality to its experience courted anew in teasing, mockery, ridicule, and derision. That characteristic of double meaning in teasing contributes to double talk, entendre or dealing modes of expression in daily life. This quality lies at the core of many forms of humor and play and may evolve into personality traits of slyness, deceit, cheating and treachery.

In the first case cited, the frightening seriousness of father's frequent diurnal rejection of the mother was belied by his bedtime lying with her. The ridicule, mockery or humiliation suffered throughout childhood by this patient, as well as Brenman's (5) and Reich's (21), became teasingly or humorously courted in later life.

Manifold strivings in interpersonal activities can be expressed in a teasing fashion. Although the mixture and intensity of the drives that comprise a particular teasing complex may be difficult to delineate, yet, in any specific instance, the distinctive teasing quality of an activity can readily be sensed by its participants.

Playful teasing of the analyst is often a progressive step in therapy, which may herald the forthright expression of strong transference feelings, especially of sadomasochistic and exhibitionistic nature. Such expression, therefore, should not be viewed solely for its defensive function. Since psychoanalytic technique involves dissolving resistances in order to elicit unconscious material, analysands who have not mastered their teasing experiences may react readily to the analysis as a teasing activity. They attempt to tease the analyst out of his relatively silent or "passive" role. Extremely stubborn resistances arising out of fears of ridicule, shame, or embarrassment can be influenced by attending more to the significant, repetitive, teasing experiences that helped fixate these in their childhood.

The phenomenon of teasing has been and continues to be

480

481

largely ignored in the psychoanalytical literature, because of primary concern with its basic components.8

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Narcissistic Object Choice in Women11

Annie Reich M.D.

Annie Reich

Annie Reich  

SUMMARY

1. The ego ideal, in contrast to the superego, is based upon a narcissistic identification with the parent, who is seen in an infantile, glorified way. Persistence of particular, grandiose ego ideals has to do with disturbances of object relationship and ego development.

2. Unsublimated sexual features of the ideal, expressed in the fantasy of becoming the paternal (or, sometimes, materal) phallus, represent a special, regressive trait.

3. Fixation on the level of imitative gesture leads to a lack of internalization in the ego ideal and constitutes the basis for the "as if" personality.

4. The impact of narcissistic injuries, such as, in women, the becoming aware of the difference of the sexes, may lead to a regressive revival of primitive, narcissistic ego ideals.

5. The externalization of such an ego ideal, and its fusion with a love object, represents a form of narcissistic object choice in women.

6. The degree of pathology of the narcissistic object choice depends on the normalcy or pathology of the ego ideal.

7. Need for identification, not infrequently in the form of ecstatic-orgastic flowing together with the idealized object, can become the basis for a subservient relationship of a woman to a man.

8. The masochistic element in such subservience is frequently based on the overcompensation of aggressive feelings against the man.

9. Idealization and identification with the idealized object may represent the only available form of substitution for the lacking ability to form object relationships.

10. A greater degree of disturbance of the ego ideal frequently goes hand in hand with an insufficiently developed superego and leads to dependence upon "public opinion" or specific third persons.

11. Sudden, aggressive demolition of idealized figures, combined with depressive lowering of self-esteem, is based upon a

predominance of aggression against the objects on whom the ego ideal is built.

responseSet.title: The Distortion of the Symbolic Process in Neurosis and Psychosis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Distortion of the Symbolic Process in Neurosis and Psychosis11

Lawrence Kubie M.D.

Lawrence Kubie

Lawrence Kubie  

SUMMARY

I have attempted to explore the implications of a new hypothesis concerning the distinction between normality and psychopathology. In doing so I have artificially segregated into two groups the forces which are at work: those forces which arise primarily through the impact of early emotional stresses on presymbolic stages of human life, and those which arise through a distortion of the symbolic process itself. I have not discussed the first group beyond pointing out that they characterize the psychopathology

84

both of subverbal animal forms and of preverbel stages of human life, whereas the latter characterizes the psychopathology which is distinctively human, whether of the child, adolescent, or adult. I have presented some evidence for the hypothesis that every unit of symbolic thinking has both an exteroceptive and an enteroceptive root: i.e., that each has a link both in the "I" world and in the "Non-I" world, and that the major distinction between the neurosis and the psychosis depends upon whether the distortion of the relationship of the symbol to what it represents is primarily at the "I" or at the "Non-I" end of the symbolic linkage, respectively. I traced certain consequences of this for that disturbance of psychological function which is called a "hallucination"; and I presented reasons for the generally overlooked fact that under appropriate circumstances certain types of hallucinatory processes fall within the realm of normal psychological functions, while others have neurotic, and still others psychotic implications. The reasons for this are correlated with the underlying thesis.

To some critics it may seen strange or even misleading that I should have focused so much of my discussion on the hallucinatory process, since in its full-blown clinical form hallucinosis is one of the relatively rare manifestations of the psychotic process. I have done so not because I believe that all psychotics hallucinate, nor that all hallucinations are psychotic; but rather because I believe that there is an unbroken continuity among all psychological manifestations, a continuity between normality, neurosis, and psychosis. If this is true, then it follows that precisely because hallucinations are one of the more extreme manifestations of the fully developed psychotic state it became essential to test my hypothesis by seeing whether it was possible to recognize and demonstrate the existence of this continuum in the hallucinatory process of normal psychology, the hallucinatory process which can occur in the neurotic, and the hallucinosis of the psychotic. Here again a close scrutiny of the more pathological processes of psychotic hallucinations throws light on the essential nature of the psychotic process itself and on the related processes of the normal.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Members of Minority Groups11

Viola Bernard M.D.

Viola Bernard

Viola Bernard  

Both analysts and their patients in this mid-twentieth century United States are participating members in a society in which intergroup tensions and cultural prejudices are prevalent. Socially ranked grouping involves membership by both analyst and patient in cultural groups of higher or lower status valuation. Inevitably, this enters into the intricate and intensive relationship between analyst and patient, to some extent. The analyst's anonymity is only relative and least maintained as regards his social class, religious, national and racial group membership. The patient's group memberships are, of course, fully known to the analyst.

The term "minority group" will be used to refer to ethnic and religious groups of relatively lowered social status whose members form a minority in relation to the United States in general, and to the local community of the patient's origin and rearing in particular. Consideration of other minorities, such as political, is not attempted here, although some of the discussion is no doubt applicable to such groups.

An increasing number of psychoanalytic investigations—some in collaboration with social scientists—have studied individuals belonging to various cultural groups. Some of these studies—many concerned with societies remote from our own—have sought to differentiate culturally determined and idiosyncratic aspects of personality, as for instance, by Kardiner's (7) concepts of basic personality structure and individual character.

1Presented at Panel "Cultural Factor in Psychoanalytic Treatment, " Annual Meeting of American Psychoanalytic Association, Cincinnati, May 1951.256

[[RunningHead]]

Countertransference and Counteridentification

Robert Fliess M.D.

Robert Fliess

Robert Fliess  

I. COUNTERTRANSFERENCE

The treatment in the literature of psychoanalysis of countertransference and counteridentification suffers from terminologic confusion, which, necessarily, causes confusion of concepts. It is the purpose of this communication to attempt to define the two terms and to oppose to the desirable attitudes of the analyst toward his patient, countertransference and counteridentification as undesirable, on the basis of their definition.

If consistency of designation is required to identify a concept unambiguously, the term "countertransference" must, by virtue of its definition be reserved for the equivalent, in the analyst, of what is termed "transference" in the patient. It is then immediately obvious that countertransference is not, as is transference, an occurrence desirable and prerequisite to the treatment, but undesirable and a hindrance. When the patient transfers infantile conflicts upon the analyst he revives them in a situation in which they may be resolved. When the analyst (counter) transfers upon the patient he revives his own conflicts, which are neither the object of the analysis, nor can they be resolved. The patient, forced to react to a situation which instead of being intrinsic is extrinsic to his past, is, in this case, in an "accident, " not a treatment. Ideally, therefore, countertransference should not occur.

But it does. The technique to abolish it becomes evident if the aforementioned definition is kept in mind. Transference, we have been taught by Freud, must be analyzed when it has become, or is about to become, a resistance; countertransference, always resistance, must always be analyzed. Its dissolution requires that the

1Its limitations should not, of course, be overlooked. Sachs (7, p. 157), for instance, has expressed himself to the extent of saying that the analyst's "own shortcomings—the deficiencies of his individual development, the gross defects of the society in which he lives, the imperfections of psychoanalytic insight and method—these will always impose serious limitations on his efficiency however incessantly he strives to overcome them.

[[RunningHead]]

The Present Status of Organized Psychoanalysis in the United States11

Robert Knight M.D.

Robert Knight

Robert Knight  

No tradition has yet been established in the American Psychoanalytic Association with regard to whether or how the outgoing President should address the membership. Some have read a scientific paper; some have spoken at the annual dinner; and some have made no special communication. I have chosen to speak at this last meeting of members at which I preside, so that my perspective might be at its optimum, marking, as it does, the penultimate meeting in fifteen years of continuous service on both the Executive Council and the two successive training organizations—the old Council on Professional Training and the present Board on Professional Standards—and including four years as Secretary and five years as President-Elect and President.

These fifteen years of the history of the American Psychoanalytic Association (1938-1953) have been characterized by both remarkable growth and stormy evolution. Because I have been an active participant, in one role or another, in the Association's affairs through this period, and because I have discovered that comparatively few members of the Association are well informed regarding these affairs, I have felt impelled to occupy myself in this Presidential Address with communicating to the members and prospective members of the Association some information, observations, and reflections concerning the present status of organized psychoanalysis in this country. I wish to sketch for you the organizational history of the Association and to relate this historical development to the evolution of the leading problem confronting the Association over the years, that of training and its

responseSet.title: On Boredom
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Boredom11

Ralph Greenson M.D.

Ralph Greenson

Ralph Greenson  

I

Boredom is a phenomenon which is easier to describe than to define. The uniqueness of the feeling of being bored seems to depend upon the coexistence of the following components: a state of dissatisfaction and a disinclination to action; a state of longing and an inability to designate what is longed for; a sense of emptiness; a passive, expectant attitude with the hope that the external world will supply the satisfaction; a distorted sense of time in which time seems to stand still. (The German word for boredom is Langeweile—which, literally translated, means "long time.")

Boredom is seen to occur in healthy people as a transient state. In the neurotic, boredom usually does not play an important role since the patient is preoccupied with anxieties, depressions, frustrations, obsessions, etc. In the psychoses, boredom proper is rarely described, but we do see similar reactions in apathy and in the depressions.

It is the purpose of this presentation to investigate, on the basis of clinical material, the various dynamic and structural factors responsible for the state of boredom. The most systematic and comprehensive analysis of this subject was made by Fenichel (3) in 1934. In that paper Fenichel demonstrated that the bored person is in a state of dammed-up instinctual tension, but the objects and aims are repressed. He described two forms of boredom, one characterized by motor calmness, the other by motor restlessness; but he found in both the same essential pathology.

1Presented in part at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Cincinnati, May, 1951.7

[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Studies on Cocaine, 1884-188711

Siegfried Bernfeld Ph.D.

Siegfried Bernfeld

Siegfried Bernfeld  

To the history of Freud's hospital years (1882-1885) (3) belongs a chapter on his advocation of cocaine, a "side interest, though a deep one" (20, pp. 23-25). In his work with cocaine Freud, for the first time, went his own way. His research under Brücke (see 2) and Meynert, as well as his work in clinical neurology, for all the talent revealed, for all the originality of detail, was basically conservative. The goals, concepts, methods, the questions asked, the answers given—all were completely within the teaching of admired and loved masters, who inspired him and pressed their stamp of approval on his efforts. The cocaine studies, on the other hand, were not sponsored by anyone. The drug, though isolated from the coca leaves in the year 1859-1860, was hardly known. Those scientists that were acquainted with it generally considered it worthless and at the same time dangerous. Rumors of its benefits were believed to be based on superstitions of wretched Peruvian Indians, accepted by gullible travelers.

In these studies Freud crosses—although only by a few steps—the narrowly drawn boundaries of the sanctioned research field to which he had previously limited himself. It must be considered as his first attempt to break out into full independence. He followed this in the next years with several stronger efforts; but it was not until the latter part of the 1890's that he was capable of pursuing his way completely alone against the "compact majority.

1Editor's note: This paper was submitted by Dr. Bernfeld in a rough draft. He had requested that the paper be returned to him for revision but unfortunately his untimely death prevented this. The manuscript was readied for publication by Mrs. Bernice Engle, of the Langley Porter Clinic, and by Mr. Peter Paret.581

[[RunningHead]]

III. the Essentials of Psychotherapy as Viewed by the Psychoanalyst

Adelaide Johnson M.D. and O. English M.D.

O. English

O. English  

Adelaide Johnson presented a paper summarizing the opinion of the panel members on the differences involved in dynamic psychotherapy and classical psychoanalysis. She expressed the panel's agreement on a principal put forth by Bernard Bandler that, "dynamic psychotherapy involves a different terrain from classical psychoanalysis, a terrain to be explored and mapped out in its own right, using the wealth of psychoanalytic concepts as a sort of compass to facilitate exploration and evolution of a rational, predictable and communicable conceptual frame of reference for treatment of masses of cases not suitable for classical psychoanalysis. Cases lie along a total spectrum with no sharp divisions in the continuum, the most superficially supported cases at one end and classical psychoanalytic cases in their special setting."

As she correlated the material gathered by the panel members for this paper Johnson noted that there was a strong tendency to focus on the countertransference problem of the analyst doing psychotherapy. "You will see this arising in the paper, over and over, intruding on all thinking about essentials in psychotherapy."

Many cases have been given in which some recent or immediate current life situation disturbed the patient's adjustment, Johnson continued, and treatment was conducted around a fairly well-encapsulated constellation of conflicts, so that with partial resolution of this conflict the patients became stabilized again. Such positive indications need much greater clarification, however, from the standpoint of the dynamics of the cases themselves and from the position of who is doing the treatment. As Chassel says,

We keep assuming that psychotherapy is a watered down procedure or is bound to be pure psychoanalysis alloyed with the baser metals of suggestion, and so on. My present thesis is that really psychodynamic psychotherapy is an approach as

[[RunningHead]]

The Ear, Listening and Hearing

Peter Knapp M.D.

Peter Knapp

Peter Knapp  

The ear, in psychodynamic investigation, is a silent area. Meticulously studied by the experimentalist, the auditory mechanism has received little attention from the psychoanalyst, although he depends on it daily. Perhaps this fact reflects a general tendency—to "view" the world in terms of our most useful perceptive tool, vision. In any dictionary of phrases, synonyms or quotations, optical references outnumber acoustic ones about four to one. Schilder's volume on the body image (27) scarcely refers to the ear. Psychoanalysts have written a great deal about language, a certain amount about sounds, including music; but there are few references to the actual acoustic apparatus, except for the studies of Abraham (1), Jones (13), and Isakower (12). This paper is an effort to extend their reports. It will discuss the role played in psychic life by the ear, as a part of the body and as a perceptual instrument.

I

My starting point was work with deafened patients (15). Gross physical damage is often inconspicuous in deafness. That fact, a source of chargrin to those who feel very crippled, can lead in others to an apparent lack of any concern with the ears as such. At a deeper level, of course, the failure of function creates feelings of mutilation, of having inferior, "bad ears.

Russell of the faculty of Boston University School of Medicine, each of whom gave permission to refer to material from a case of theirs from the clinic and private services of the Massachusetts Memorial Hospital.672

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fetishism11

Robert Bak M.D.

Robert Bak

Robert Bak  

Fetishism, as Freud (6) has shown, is a special solution of the castration threat. This solution is carried out by the mechanism of the denial of a part of reality consisting of the refusal to acknowledge the lack of a penis in women. Thus, the fetish is a substitute for the female penis (i.e., of the mother). It may be a symbol of the penis or it may represent the object of "last moment in which the woman could still be regarded as phallic." The choice of the fetish often is influenced by the return of repressed coprophilic smell desires (3). The main significance of the fetish is due to its protective values as a "safe-guard against the danger of castration." The reality of this danger is recognized at the site of the female genital but it is repudiated simultaneously and the two conflicting perceptions in the denial constitute a split in the ego (7). Freud emphasized throughout that the core of the problem lies in the horror of the female genital and he also posed the question why the trauma of the sight of penislessness is overcome in the majority of cases but in some it leads to fetishism or homosexuality.

In what follows we shall make an attempt to contribute to this problem. Freud had indicated that the decisive screen memories of fetishists usually date not earlier than from the ages of five or six. Therefore, earlier experiences must determine the choice of the fetish. In other words, whereas the fetishistic defense of castration anxiety emerges in the phallic phase, it is predetermined by prephallic experiences.

1Read before the Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Atlantic City, N. J., May 9, 1952.285

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Effect of the Structure of the Ego on Psychoanalytic Technique

K. Eissler M.D.

K. Eissler

K. Eissler  

The subject matter of this paper is closely related to a problem which has occupied the minds of analysts for decades. It is within the scope of the question which Freud raised at the Berlin Psychoanalytic Convention (1922) when he asked: "What is the relationship between psychoanalytic technique and psychoanalytic theory?" Freud's question encompassed a vast area, only one portion of which pertains to the subject matter of this paper.

Freud's question of 1922 will interest us today particularly in conjunction with the structure of the ego. During the last two decades a certain sentiment has spread which might be formulated as follows: If our knowledge of the structure of the ego were complete, then a variety of techniques—ideally adapted to the requirements of the individual disturbance—could be perfected; thus we could assure definite mastery of the ego over those areas in which it had suffered defeat, that is to say, assure complete recovery. Like all sentiments, this one does not adequately reflect objective reality, but it is probably correct to say that greatly deepened, almost complete, insight into the structure of the ego would multiply the clinical effectiveness of psychoanalytic techniques. The pessimist's claim, however, must be conceded: Full knowledge of the structure of the ego would, no doubt, make the task of changing that structure appear in its true and gigantic proportion, inducing us as a consequence to withdraw modestly from such heroic attempts.

Before delving into the subject matter, I want to exclude two variables which have great bearing on psychoanalytic techniques.

Studies on the Nature of Suggestion—Part II the Transference Meaning of Giving Suggestions11

Charles Fisher Ph.D.

Charles Fisher

Charles Fisher  

In Part I of this investigation (10) it was shown that patients under analysis accepted suggestions to dream in 68% of 95 experiments, and that the content of the suggestion influenced the content of the dream produced. Even when a dream was not produced the influence of the suggestion could be demonstrated in other behavioral manifestations. The various categories of result, such as acting out and symptom formation as substitutes for dreaming, were discussed in terms of compromise formations between unconscious impulses activated by the dream suggestions and defensive functions of the ego. The failure to dream was related to the development of overt or latent hostility, with special stress on the possible role of the arousal of oral sadism. It was demonstrated that patients in analysis show a capacity for accepting dream suggestions, which approaches that of hypnotic subjects, and that when given suggestions their behavior resembles that of hypnotic subjects. Increased suggestibility was discussed as one of the properties of states of induced regression, among which are to be included the hypnotic and analytic relationships.

Louis Linn M.D.

Louis Linn

Louis Linn  

SUMMARY

1. In the patient who is characterologically predisposed to the use of denial, interferences with perception facilitate denial.

2. Conversely, the need to deny can result in functional disturbances in perception (inexact perception).

3. The relationship of denial to other defense mechanisms is discussed.

4. Some general psychoanalytic implications of these data are considered.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Interchangeability of Phallus and Female Genital11

Leo Rangell M.D.

Leo Rangell

Leo Rangell  

The usual direction of psychic displacement, from an area charged with conflictful emotion, is toward an object or activity of relatively neutral or uncharged emotional value. In this way, the genitalia, in the language of the dynamic unconscious, might be referred to by displacement to an innocuous object or to another less libidinized part. The symbolism, for example, of other parts being equivalent to the phallus, such as the body, nose, arms, etc., is well known, as is displacement of the female genital to such areas as the ear, nostrils, or anus.

In contrast to this type of remote or symbolic representation, however, it has been less alluded to that the genital itself of either sex can be used, in fantasy or act, to express or represent genitality of the opposite sex. That is, bisexuality, the repressed half of which is usually expressed by reference to a distant part or object, can also be and often is expressed at the source itself. It is much akin to the counterphobic attitude. And it is similar to the criminal who feels that the place he is least likely to be looked for is at the scene of the crime itself. Elements of this fantasy of the interchangeability of male to female sexual organ and vice versa are not uncommon. Either organ can be used unconsciously in either sexual function, and careful eliciting of these component functional usages is clinically valuable. It is the purpose of this communication to bring together some brief clinical data illustrative of this duality and thus to bring this observation into sharper focus. The following clinical excerpts are in point.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Defenses in Symptom Formation11

George Gero M.D.

George Gero

George Gero  

SUMMARY

An attempt was made to understand some aspects of symptom formation, the interplay and dialectic of the defenses which are involved in it.

The formation of phobic symptomatology was studied. The phobic displacement was characterized as a biphasic act. Inhibitory and releasing activities of the ego are prerequisites for the appearance of phobic displacement. This idea suggests that an external perception activates not only anxiety-loaded memory traces but fantasies and drive patterns as well. The phobic anxiety is a response to those drives which are released toward the external perceptions. Such a release is viewed as an unconscious ego activity.

To understand better the defenses' role in the formation of

102

symptoms it is advantageous to distinguish between defenses before and after the symptom appeared. The inhibitory acts, that is to say, the repression of drives and fantasies growing out of the oedipal situation comprise the defensive steps before the symptom is established, whereas the phobic avoidance is a defense against the already crystallized symptom.

The role of defenses was then investigated in another symptom, in obsessive ideas. The question was raised as to which defensive activities are responsible for the formal character of the symptom. The obsessive idea was characterized as the perception of a highly cathected but desexualized impulse. The desexualization of the impulse which appears as an obsessive thought is accomplished through individually variable defensive activities. Essential parts of the sexual fantasy underlying the obsessive thought are repressed. If the fantasy appears in consciousness it has to be kept pale and abstract; detailed imagination is inhibited. Bodily sensations which the fantasy would activate are blocked. There is a reliable clinical indicator which serves as a criterion for the detection of the defenses operating in the obsessive thought. At any time if those defenses are interfered with in the analysis, anxiety is apt to be experienced.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Federn's Ego Psychology and its Application to Agoraphobia11

Edoardo Weiss M.D.

Edoardo Weiss

Edoardo Weiss  

I. THE DEVELOPMENT OF FEDERN'S EGO PSYCHOLOGY

Federn's personality is expressed in everything he achieved in psychological investigation. His specific scientific aim was to clarify some of the components of our ego, of our own individual experience. The feeling of one's own being is the most fascinating field of psychology: our sensing of our own bodily and mental existence is even more mysterious than the origin and nature of life itself.

Indeed, Federn did not approach this very problem; he never said that he could explain the ego Erlebnis itself. What he did do was to apply consistently the economic, dynamic, and topographic viewpoints to the study of the dynamic functioning of the ego experience in its normal and morbid aspects.

He investigated the modes of ego operation, utilizing the material from the most accurate introspections of healthy and diseased persons. The knowledge thus acquired induced him to postulate a coherent ego cathexis unity as the metapsychological, dynamic basis of the ego experience.

To appreciate Federn's original contributions to ego psychology and to the understanding of psychoses, one must consider them in the light of Freud's psychoanalytic concepts, for Federn's ego psychology was a logical construct developed from his own intellectual independence and his unshakable loyalty to Freud. His intellectual independence made unavoidable certain differences between some of Freud's concepts and his own.

1Adapted from a paper read December 13, 1952, before a circle of former students of Paul Federn in New York on the occasion of the publication of his book, Ego Psychology and the Psychoses, New York: Basic Books, Inc., 1952.614

[[RunningHead]]

The Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

Robert Knight M.D.

Robert Knight

Robert Knight  

With this issue—Volume I, Number I—we announce the birth of a new psychoanalytic journal. Its legitimate siblings are quite mature and, when consulted prior to the conception of the newcomer, all expressed a genuine welcome into which crept no observable trace of anticipatory sibling rivalry. We are glad to join the respected company of The Psychoanalytic Review, aged 40, The International Journal of Psycho-Analysis, aged 34, and The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, aged 22.

This is to be the "official" journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association. Such a designation does not mean to imply that the other American journals are unofficial in their scientific publications; it simply means that the new journal will publish, in addition to well-selected psychoanalytic papers, the official proceedings of the American Psychoanalytic Association. During the forty-two years of the Association its proceedings have, until recently, not been published at all. The Bulletin of the Association was published for three years, 1937 to 1940, and then abandoned until 1948 when it was revived in a new format. Since 1948 it has been published continuously, growing more voluminous each year as its editors attempted to include both scientific material and official reports of an Association which has grown in fifteen years from five to fourteen societies, and from 158 members to over triple that number. It was inevitable that the rapidly growing Association would need an official organ, and preliminary discussions led the Executive Council and the Association to approve of planning for the new journal in May 1951, and to final acceptance of the plans in December 1951.

The Journal is official in other senses, also. The Administrative Board consists of the current officers of the Association and the Editor.

[[RunningHead]]

Panel Reports—midwinter Meeting, 1952—I. the Traditional Psychoanalytic Technique and its Variations

Douglas Orr M.D. and Elizabeth Zetzel M.D.

Elizabeth Zetzel

Elizabeth Zetzel  

Although all the contributors to this symposium concentrated on its main topic—technique—the discussion clearly illustrated a point raised by several participants to the effect that it is impossible to separate technique from the underlying theoretical premises on which it is based. In this discussion, moreover, the theoretical premises coming under review covered a very wide range, including the very cornerstone on which all analytic development has been based—namely, our fundamental conception of the nature of neurosis, various points of view as to the nature of the therapeutic process in analysis, and illustrating how closely such views correlate with our conception of and attitude toward the transference neurosis.

Phyllis Greenacre opened the meeting with a paper described by one of the discussants as containing all the basic facts concerning transference, its utilization, and the value and significance of practical arrangements, which should be imparted to every candidate about to embark on his first control case. This is certainly true. On the other hand, a review of this paper, in the light of the day's discussion, suggests that it is in fact deceptively simple. In the first section, for example, Greenacre describes the essentials of the transference relationship—showing the inevitable resemblance (and of course significant differences) between the transference situation and the mother-child relationship. In so doing, she makes it abundantly clear that she regards the dependent aspects of the transference (regressive though they may be) as integral and unavoidable—and not as an undesirable development which should be minimized.

[[RunningHead]]

II. Problems of Identification

Robert Fliess M.D. and Herbert Wiggers M.D.

Herbert Wiggers

Herbert Wiggers  

This panel was opened with a paper by Ralph Greenson entitled "The Struggle Against Identification," "based on the clinical findings in a group of patients in whom the struggle against identification was of great significance in understanding the clinical and theoretical picture." The author postulated that while fantasies of physical incorporation in infancy resulted in psychic introjection, such introjection might or might not bring about an identification and a transformation of the self into the image of the external object.

He presented four cases, the symptomatology of which was based on a denial of early identifications based on fantasies of oral incorporation. Thus, a woman whose mother was of German extraction refused to use her "mother tongue," while still another consciously dreaded following her mother's pathways of irresponsibility and promiscuity. These patients both experienced swings of mood between states of empty boredom during which they denied their identification with their mothers, and aggressive, promiscuous, overactivity during which they acted out in a fashion identical with their mothers'.

The third patient, a parasitologist, consciously hated his father to the extent of indulging in the fantasy that he was someone else's son. At the same time he suffered from repetitive dreams of having a worm inside his body. Periodic eruptions of aggressive, destructive, sexual behavior were identical with the behavior he loathed in his father. "A chronic good mood was a screen affect which was found to be a countercathexis against an underlying depressive, aggressive state, made possible by the extensive use of negation and denial of perceptions."

The fourth patient was unable to recognize in himself characteristics related to the performance of the sexual act "which his mother despised in his father." Whenever he thought that he was like his father he experienced a feeling of depersonalization except in one activity, sexual intercourse.

[[RunningHead]]

Cultural Factors in Psychoanalytic Therapy11

George Devereux Ph.D.

George Devereux

George Devereux  

A technical discussion of the influence of cultural factors upon the course and technique of psychoanalytic therapy must begin with a preliminary analysis of the nature of culture, and of its fate in mental health, psychological illness, and psychoanalytic therapy. Since some of this material has already been touched upon in another study (11), it will be presented here only in a more or less axiomatic form.

1. CULTURE AS A CHARACTERISTIC HUMAN TRAIT

Definition of Terms: In the following discussion we differentiate between homo sapiens or genus homo as a biological organism and man as a human being.

1. Homo sapiens is the current end product of an evolutionary process toward a high degree of differentiation and individualization. The principal and uniquely characteristic trait of homo sapiens —the "constant of human nature"—is the extreme plasticity and variability of his behavior.

2. The aforementioned four characteristics of genus homo: differentiation, individualization, plasticity, and variability of behavior, represent a unitary biological potentiality which is actualized in the acquisition of a distinctively human psyche and of culture.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Disorder of Depression and Elation—A Clinical Study of the Changes from One State to the Other11

Gregory Rochlin M.D.

Gregory Rochlin

Gregory Rochlin  

There is a large literature relating to depressive states and elation and to some extent it has been critically reviewed. It is fair to say, however, that little systematic or consecutive study of the problems involved has been made, notwithstanding the fact that they have been approached from a great many directions. One consecutive sequence of investigations can be observed in the work of Abraham (1), Freud (5), Helene Deutsch (2) and Bertram Lewin (9). Perhaps the best critical examination of these studies is in Lewin's recent valuable work, The Psychoanalysis of Elation(10).

The foundation of our present psychoanalytic understanding of these commonplace disorders is the classic papers by Freud and Abraham. The two most promising trends in the recent study of the disorders are in the work of Helene Deutsch and of Bertram Lewin. Deutsch is concerned chiefly with ego phenomena which these disorders show in the later phases of development, whereas Lewin concentrates on the "psychological matrix of the defenses, their antecedant anlage in the early stages of the psyche" (10, p. 10). The two authors complement each other and have widened the basis of our understanding.

Clinical reports of these states usually concentrate on one phase or the other, on depression or on elation. Very few analysts have studied the relation between the states or the transition from one to the other.

1Read before the American Psychoanalytic Association, Atlantic City, N. J., May 9th, 1952.438

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Orality in the Hysterical Personality11

Judd Marmor M.D.

Judd Marmor

Judd Marmor  

The subject of hysteria is particularly significant for psychoanalysts, for it was in the process of the study and treatment of this condition that Freud discovered the method of psychoanalysis and developed many of its basic theories. The concepts of fixation and regression grew out of these early studies, as did the formulation that hysterics are fixated at the phallic or early genital phase of libidinal development. Over the years a psychoanalytic characterology emerged which assumed increasing clinical importance. Wilhelm Reich in his Charakteranalyse(12) was the first to state what had become implicit in the theory and practice of psychoanalysis but had not until then been explicitly expressed—namely, that a neurotic character structure underlies every symptom neurosis, and that the optimum goal of psychoanalytic therapy must be not merely the eradication of the neurotic symptom but the changing of the neurotic character structure. It is the underlying character structure of hysteria which is the concern of this communication.

It should be emphasized at the outset that we are admittedly dealing with an abstraction. There is no pure hysterical personality any more than there is a pure compulsive personality. In personality development, residuals of earlier stages of growth can invariably be found interspersed with the characteristics of later stages. Nevertheless, broad clinical differences do exist, and there is some value in attempting to define the main patterns which are associated with these differences.

[[RunningHead]]

The Problem of Latent Psychosis11

Gustav Bychowski M.D.

Gustav Bychowski

Gustav Bychowski  

The title of this communication may be misleading, since in reality there is not just one but many problems related to latent psychosis. Moreover, it should be pointed out from the very outset that what is meant by latent psychosis is by no means a merely dialectical psychotic potentiality of every individual. According to such a definition we are all virtual psychotics just as we are all potential criminals.

On the contrary, we deal with a clinical reality. Bleuler (1, p. 239) spoke of latent schizophrenia as:

The most frequent form, although admittedly these people hardly ever come for treatment. … In this form we can see in nuce all the symptoms and all the combinations which are present in the manifest types of the disease. Irritable, odd, moody, withdrawn or exaggeratedly punctual people arouse among other things, the suspicion of being schizophrenic. Often one discovers concealed catatonic or paranoid symptoms, and exacerbations occurring in later life demonstrate that every form of this disease may take a latent course.

As to the disposition for a depressive psychosis, it has been even less sharply defined by descriptive psychiatry.

A comparison of these early concepts with our present ideas shows a line of development based on the ever wider use of tools provided by psychoanalysis.

1Read at the Annual Convention of the American Psychoanalytic Association in Atlantic City, May, 1952.484

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Masturbation and Symptom Formation11

Jacob Arlow M.D.

Jacob Arlow

Jacob Arlow  

From the point of view of the dynamic, defensive functioning of the psychic apparatus, masturbation and symptoms have much in common. Both demonstrate how the ego operates in attempting to achieve a satisfactory solution of the conflicting claims of the instincts, the pressure of the superego, and the demands of reality. Masturbation and symptom formation both portray the end product of a complicated and overdetermined set of mental operations in the ego, operations which proceed according to the principle of multiple functioning (17). These elements of similarity may be taken as the starting point in discussing the intimate relationship between masturbation and the formation of certain psychoneurotic symptoms.

In an early paper written before he had elaborated his dual concept of instincts and the structural organization of the psyche, Freud (9) commented on the connection between the unconscious fantasy associated with hysterical symptoms and the conscious fantasy connected with masturbation. "It is actually identical with the fantasy which served the person in his sexual gratification during the period of masturbation. … Such unconscious fantasies are the immediate precursors in the mind of a whole series of hysterical symptoms. The hysterical symptoms are nothing but the unconscious fantasies made manifest by 'conversion' and in so far as the symptoms are of a somatic kind, they are often enough drawn from within the range of sexual feelings and motor innervations that regularly accompanied the fantasy while it was still conscious.

1Presented at the Midwinter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York City, December 8, 1951.45
